instruments: Payment Services Act 2019 (Act 2 of 2019, 'PS Act') -- Singapore's base activity-based licensing statute, administered by MAS, under which 'digital payment token service' (DPT service) is one of the regulated payment services requiring a licence (Standard Payment Institution or Major Payment Institution, reported as two of the PS Act's three licence tiers -- alongside a Money-Changing Licence -- by multiple independent secondary/law-firm sources); widely and consistently reported, including by MAS's own 2 April 2024 media release cited below, as having commenced 28 January 2020. This research could not itself directly fetch Singapore Statutes Online's consolidated PS Act text this session to independently confirm that commencement date or the PS Act's own licence-category provisions from primary statutory text: sso.agc.gov.sg returned a live-verified HTTP 403 CloudFront block on every path tried, including robots.txt. Direct fetch of the Payment Services (Amendment) Bill (Bill No. 41/2020, parliament.gov.sg, described immediately below) does independently confirm, from genuine primary text, that 'major payment institution' is a term used in the principal Act distinct from 'licensee' generally (e.g. amended section 23 repeatedly distinguishes 'a major payment institution' from 'any prescribed licensee (other than a major payment institution)') -- consistent with, though not on its own sufficient to confirm full detail of, the widely-reported two-tier structure -- see open_items

Payment Services (Amendment) Bill, Bill No. 41/2020 (read first time 2 November 2020, became the Payment Services (Amendment) Act 2021 on commencement) -- confirmed by direct fetch of the genuine PDF from parliament.gov.sg. Clause 7(e) of the Bill, fetched and quoted verbatim, inserts new sub-paragraphs into the PS Act First Schedule's definition of 'digital payment token service', immediately after existing (unamended, and so not independently reproduced in this Bill) sub-paragraphs (a) and (b): new sub-paragraph (c) covers 'any service of inducing or attempting to induce any person to enter into or to offer to enter into any agreement for or with a view to buying or selling any digital payment token in exchange for any money or any other digital payment token' -- the exchange/dealing-facilitation and solicitation activity that is the operative licensing hook for a DPT exchange or trading-platform business; new sub-paragraphs (d)-(i) add transmission-facilitation, custody/safeguarding and instruction-execution services. The Bill's structure itself (clause 7(d), inserting a new definition 'immediately after the definition of "digital payment token exchange" in paragraph 3') independently confirms that 'digital payment token exchange' was already a separately defined term in the original, unamended 2019 First Schedule -- though this session's fetch did not include (because the Bill does not reproduce) that pre-existing definition's exact wording, nor the original sub-paragraphs (a)/(b) of 'digital payment token service' that the amendment left unchanged -- see open_items

MAS media release, 'MAS Expands Scope of Regulated Payment Services; Introduces User Protection Requirements for Digital Payment Token Service Providers', 2 April 2024 -- confirmed by direct fetch of the genuine PDF via the Singapore Press Centre (sgpc.gov.sg), since every path tried against www.mas.gov.sg this session, including this exact document's own -/media/ PDF URL, returned an identical 853,817-byte 'Maintenance' HTML page, live-verified today. States verbatim that the amendments to the PS Act and its subsidiary legislation 'take effect in stages from 4 April 2024' and bring within licensing scope, among other activities: '(ii) Facilitation of the transmission of DPTs between accounts and facilitation of the exchange of DPTs, even where the service provider does not come into possession of the moneys or DPTs' -- the direct primary-sourced confirmation that DPT-exchange-facilitation activity, even by an intermediary that never takes custody, requires a PS Act licence from that date. Sets transitional deadlines measured from 4 April 2024: 30-day MAS notification, 6-month licence application, 9-month AML/CFT attestation report by a qualified external auditor, all applicable to any entity 'currently conducting activities under the PS Act's expanded scope' -- exchange/trading-platform operators among them; entities that do not meet these 'are required to cease the activities when the amendments come into effect'

MAS media release, 'MAS Strengthens Regulatory Measures for Digital Payment Token Services', 23 November 2023 -- confirmed by direct fetch of the genuine PDF via sgpc.gov.sg. Publishes MAS's final tranche of consultation responses on business-conduct and consumer-access measures for DPT service providers, quoted verbatim: business conduct requires providers to '[i]dentify, mitigate and clearly disclose potential and actual conflicts of interest', '[p]ublish policies, procedures and criteria that govern the listing of a DPT', and '[e]stablish effective policies and procedures to handle customer complaints and resolve disputes' -- the listing-policy and conflicts-disclosure requirements that apply specifically to a DPT trading venue's own operation; consumer-access measures require providers to discourage retail speculation by determining customer risk awareness, not offering trading incentives, not providing financing/margin/leverage transactions, not accepting locally issued credit-card payments, and limiting DPT value in net-worth assessments; a technology/cyber-risk requirement for 'high availability and recoverability' of critical systems is also introduced; all measures were stated to 'take effect in phases from mid-2024' through regulations and guidelines. Ms Ho Hern Shin, MAS Deputy Managing Director (Financial Supervision), is quoted urging consumers 'to not deal with unregulated entities, including those based overseas' -- MAS's own contemporaneous framing of the unlicensed-platform risk that the Investor Alert List mechanism (below) is designed to flag

MAS Guidelines on Provision of Digital Payment Token Services to the Public [PS-G02], issued 17 January 2022 -- this research could not directly fetch PS-G02 itself or any primary MAS/sgpc.gov.sg mirror of its issuance this session: every www.mas.gov.sg path tried, including this guideline's own landing-page URL, served the same 'Maintenance' bot-block page, and no sgpc.gov.sg PDF mirror of a January 2022 MAS release on this subject was located despite a search. Its existence, title, issuance date and general effect (restricting DPT service providers from advertising or marketing DPT services to the general public in Singapore -- e.g. in public transport, physical public spaces, mainstream and social media, or through third parties such as social-media influencers -- while continuing to permit advertising through a provider's own corporate websites, apps and official channels; effective immediately on issue) are stated here only as consistently and independently corroborated by multiple secondary sources (contemporaneous Reuters, Straits Times and Channel News Asia news reporting, and Allen & Gledhill, Morgan Lewis and Lexology law-firm client alerts, all dated on or shortly after 17 January 2022), not from independently read primary guideline text -- see open_items

MAS media release, 'MAS Finalises Stablecoin Regulatory Framework', 15 August 2023 -- confirmed by direct fetch of the genuine PDF via sgpc.gov.sg. Cited in this pillar only for its direct, primary-sourced confirmation of the Investor Alert List (IAL) mechanism's operation, quoted verbatim: 'Any person that misrepresents a token as an "MAS-regulated stablecoin", may be subject to penalties under MAS' stablecoin regulatory framework, and placed on MAS' Investor Alert List' -- confirming, from genuine primary text, that MAS operates a named 'Investor Alert List' as a live consumer-warning and (alongside financial penalties or imprisonment) enforcement-adjacent mechanism; this specific release's own IAL trigger (misrepresenting stablecoin regulatory status) differs from the unlicensed-DPT-exchange-operation trigger reported for exchanges/trading platforms (see the Investor Alert List / Binance.com entry below), and this pillar does not extend this release's stablecoin-specific finding to assert IAL's exchange-specific use as independently primary-confirmed. The stablecoin regulatory framework's own substance is tracked in full in the Stablecoins pillar and not restated here

MAS media release, 'MAS Introduces Initiatives to Promote Responsible Online Financial Content, Issues Advisory Letters to Content Creators', 25 September 2025 -- confirmed by direct fetch of the genuine PDF via sgpc.gov.sg. Announces new 'Guidelines on Standards of Conduct for Digital Advertising Activities', effective from 25 March 2026, which 'apply to all financial institutions and their appointed third parties, including online content creators', requiring 'managing the challenges and limitations of digital media, ensuring clear disclosures, as well as setting appropriate policies and procedures to monitor digital advertising activities'; also announces a joint MAS/Advertising Standards Authority of Singapore guide, '7 must-knows when sharing financial information online', and advisory letters issued to five content creators 'who may have provided financial advice without a licence'. This release's own text, read in full, does not name digital payment tokens, cryptocurrency, DPT service providers or PS-G02 specifically -- DPT service providers are financial institutions licensed by MAS and so fall within the Guidelines' stated general scope, but this pillar does not assert that the Guidelines specifically target, supersede or amend PS-G02's DPT-specific advertising restrictions, since the release itself draws no such express link -- see open_items

Investor Alert List (IAL) and its application to unlicensed DPT service providers -- mas.gov.sg/investor-alert-list itself returned the same 'Maintenance' bot-block page as every other www.mas.gov.sg path tried this session, so this research could not directly fetch or quote the IAL landing page or any specific listing decision. Multiple independent secondary sources (Reuters, The Straits Times, Channel News Asia, The Business Times and the Financial Times, all dated on or around 2 September 2021) consistently report that MAS placed Binance.com on the IAL on 2 September 2021 and directed it to stop providing DPT services to Singapore residents without a PS Act licence. Consistent with editorial policy that secondary sources may corroborate but never solely establish a claim, this pillar records the episode as secondary-reported background only, not as an independently primary-confirmed fact -- see open_items

Financial Services and Markets Act 2022 (FSM Act), Part 9 -- the Digital Token Service Provider (DTSP) licensing regime, commenced 30 June 2025, requiring a licence for token-related dealing/exchange/custody-facilitation services performed from Singapore solely for customers located outside Singapore. This pillar records only that the regime exists and that MAS 'will generally not issue' such a licence given elevated money-laundering risk, per MAS's own 6 June 2025 media release (fetched via sgpc.gov.sg); the regime's licensing/scope mechanics are tracked in full in the Dealing, Custody, Advisory & Management pillar and its AML/CFT rationale in the AML/CFT & Enforcement pillar, and are not restated here to avoid duplicating citations
standing_summary: Singapore licenses cryptocurrency exchange and trading-platform activity as one strand of MAS's single, activity-based Payment Services Act 2019 (PS Act) regime, rather than under a dedicated exchange or VATP statute. The PS Act -- widely and consistently reported, including by MAS's own 2 April 2024 media release fetched below, as having commenced 28 January 2020 -- created a licensable 'digital payment token service' (DPT service) category, administered through a licence-tier structure (a Standard Payment Institution licence for lower-volume business and a Major Payment Institution licence above prescribed thresholds, alongside a separate Money-Changing Licence for non-DPT business) that multiple independent secondary/legal-commentary sources describe consistently, though this research could not itself directly fetch Singapore Statutes Online's consolidated PS Act text this session to independently confirm the licence-tier thresholds from primary statutory text: sso.agc.gov.sg returned a live-verified HTTP 403 CloudFront block on every path tried, including robots.txt. This research did, however, directly fetch and quote the Payment Services (Amendment) Bill (Bill No. 41/2020, parliament.gov.sg, which became the Payment Services (Amendment) Act 2021 on commencement), whose own structure confirms two things about exchange-specific scope from primary text: first, that 'digital payment token exchange' was already a separately defined term in the original 2019 First Schedule (the Bill inserts a new definition immediately after it, without needing to amend it), meaning exchange activity was licensable from the PS Act's original 2020 commencement; and second, that the amendment's clause 7(e) expanded the 'digital payment token service' definition with new sub-paragraphs covering, among others, 'any service of inducing or attempting to induce any person to enter into or to offer to enter into any agreement for or with a view to buying or selling any digital payment token in exchange for any money or any other digital payment token' -- an exchange/solicitation-facilitation activity captured even where, per MAS's own 2 April 2024 media release (fetched as a genuine PDF via the Singapore Press Centre, sgpc.gov.sg, since www.mas.gov.sg itself served an identical 'Maintenance' bot-block page to every path tried this session, including that document's own URL), 'the service provider does not come into possession of the moneys or DPTs'. That 2 April 2024 release confirms these amendments, together with the amended Payment Services Regulations and Notices, 'take effect in stages from 4 April 2024', with transitional deadlines running from that date: 30 days to notify MAS, six months to submit a licence application, and nine months for an external-auditor-completed AML/CFT attestation report, all applicable to any entity already conducting the newly regulated activities, on pain of having to cease those activities if the deadlines are missed. Beyond the licensing gate itself, MAS has also, through consultation, set exchange/platform-operation-specific conduct standards: MAS's 23 November 2023 media release (also fetched via sgpc.gov.sg) confirms, quoted verbatim, that DPT service providers must '[p]ublish policies, procedures and criteria that govern the listing of a DPT' and '[i]dentify, mitigate and clearly disclose potential and actual conflicts of interest' (business conduct), and must discourage retail speculation by not offering trading incentives, not providing financing/margin/leverage transactions, not accepting locally issued credit-card payments, and limiting DPT value in net-worth assessments (consumer access) -- both sets of measures 'to take effect in phases from mid-2024' -- alongside a requirement for 'high availability and recoverability' of critical systems. MAS Deputy Managing Director Ho Hern Shin is quoted in that same release urging consumers 'to not deal with unregulated entities, including those based overseas' -- MAS's own framing of the unlicensed-platform risk that its Investor Alert List (IAL) is designed to address. On marketing restrictions specifically: MAS's Guidelines on Provision of Digital Payment Token Services to the Public [PS-G02], issued 17 January 2022, are widely and consistently reported by independent secondary sources (contemporaneous Reuters, Straits Times and Channel News Asia reporting, and Allen & Gledhill, Morgan Lewis and Lexology law-firm alerts) as restricting DPT service providers from advertising or marketing DPT services to the general public -- in public transport, physical public spaces, mainstream or social media, or through third parties such as influencers -- while permitting advertising through a provider's own corporate channels; this research could not itself directly fetch PS-G02 or locate any sgpc.gov.sg mirror of its issuance this session (every www.mas.gov.sg path tried, including the guideline's own landing page, served the same 'Maintenance' block), so PS-G02's content is stated here as secondary-corroborated background only, not as independently read primary text -- see open_items. A newer, broader development in the same space, genuinely fetched this session, is MAS's 25 September 2025 media release announcing 'Guidelines on Standards of Conduct for Digital Advertising Activities' (effective 25 March 2026), which 'apply to all financial institutions and their appointed third parties, including online content creators'; because MAS-licensed DPT service providers are financial institutions, they fall within this general scope, but the release's own text does not mention digital payment tokens, cryptocurrency or PS-G02 by name, so this pillar does not assert that it specifically supersedes or is targeted at DPT-service advertising -- that relationship should be independently re-verified once confirmed. On the Investor Alert List mechanism itself: this research directly fetched and can confirm, from MAS's own 15 August 2023 stablecoin-framework media release (via sgpc.gov.sg), that MAS operates a named 'Investor Alert List' and that misrepresentation of regulated status 'may be subject to penalties ... and placed on MAS' Investor Alert List' -- genuine primary confirmation that the IAL exists and functions as a live consumer-warning and enforcement-adjacent tool, albeit confirmed in a stablecoin-mislabeling context rather than an unlicensed-exchange context specifically. mas.gov.sg/investor-alert-list itself returned the same bot-block page as every other MAS path tried this session, so the IAL's specific application to unlicensed DPT exchanges could not be independently fetched; this research instead found multiple independent, mutually consistent secondary reports (Reuters, The Straits Times, Channel News Asia, The Business Times, Financial Times, all dated on or around 2 September 2021) that MAS placed Binance.com on the IAL on 2 September 2021 and directed it to cease providing DPT services to Singapore residents without a PS Act licence -- recorded here as secondary-reported background only, consistent with the editorial rule that media reporting may corroborate but never solely establish a factual claim, and not independently primary-confirmed this session. Separately, since 30 June 2025, the Financial Services and Markets Act 2022 Part 9 Digital Token Service Provider (DTSP) regime creates a further licensing gate for token dealing/exchange/custody-facilitation services performed from Singapore solely for customers outside Singapore, which MAS's own 6 June 2025 media release states it 'will generally not' license, given elevated money-laundering risk; this pillar records only that fact. This pillar covers the DPT-service exchange/trading-platform licensing gate itself (the PS Act's licence-tier structure, the 2021 amendment's widened 'digital payment token service' definition and its 4 April 2024 commencement, PS-G02's public-advertising restrictions, and the Investor Alert List as applied to unlicensed exchanges) and does not restate: custody/safeguarding-of-assets substance and the FSM Act Part 9 DTSP regime's licensing/scope mechanics, both tracked in the Dealing, Custody, Advisory & Management pillar; or AML/CFT-specific rules (MAS Notice PSN02, the DTSP regime's AML/CFT rationale and Notice FSM-N27) and enforcement precedent, tracked in the AML/CFT & Enforcement pillar -- these are cross-referenced rather than duplicated here to avoid repeating citations across pillars.

key_links.label: Parliament of Singapore: Payment Services (Amendment) Bill, Bill No. 41/2020 (read first time 2 November 2020) -- official PDF; source of the widened 'digital payment token service' definition (clause 7(e)) quoted above; fetch-verified live this session

Singapore Press Centre (sgpc.gov.sg): MAS media release, 'MAS Expands Scope of Regulated Payment Services; Introduces User Protection Requirements for Digital Payment Token Service Providers', 2 April 2024 -- official PDF, fetch-verified live this session; source of the DPT-exchange-facilitation licensing scope and transitional-deadline quotes

Singapore Press Centre (sgpc.gov.sg): MAS media release, 'MAS Strengthens Regulatory Measures for Digital Payment Token Services', 23 November 2023 -- official PDF, fetch-verified live this session; source of the listing-policy, conflicts-of-interest and consumer-access quotes

Singapore Press Centre (sgpc.gov.sg): MAS media release, 'MAS Finalises Stablecoin Regulatory Framework', 15 August 2023 -- official PDF, fetch-verified live this session; source of the Investor Alert List mechanism quote (cited here for the IAL only; full stablecoin content tracked in the Stablecoins pillar)

Singapore Press Centre (sgpc.gov.sg): MAS media release, 'MAS Introduces Initiatives to Promote Responsible Online Financial Content, Issues Advisory Letters to Content Creators', 25 September 2025 -- official PDF, fetch-verified live this session; source of the Guidelines on Standards of Conduct for Digital Advertising Activities quotes

MAS: Guidelines on Provision of Digital Payment Token Services to the Public [PS-G02] -- link only; every path tried on www.mas.gov.sg, including this guideline's own landing page, served an identical 'Maintenance' bot-block page to this project's honest User-Agent and WebFetch alike, verified this session; cannot currently satisfy the quote-authenticity gate

MAS: Investor Alert List -- link only; www.mas.gov.sg served the same 'Maintenance' bot-block page on this path this session; MAS's operation of the IAL mechanism is instead corroborated via the genuinely-fetched 15 August 2023 stablecoin media release above, but this landing page (and any specific listing, including Binance.com's reported 2 September 2021 addition) could not be independently fetched or quoted

Singapore Statutes Online (sso.agc.gov.sg): Payment Services Act 2019 -- link only; returned an HTTP 403 CloudFront error on every path tried this session (including robots.txt); cannot currently satisfy the quote-authenticity gate, listed here for reference only

open_items: PS-G02 (Guidelines on Provision of Digital Payment Token Services to the Public, 17 January 2022) could not be directly fetched this session: every www.mas.gov.sg path tried, including the guideline's own landing page, served an identical 'Maintenance' bot-block page, and no sgpc.gov.sg PDF mirror of its issuance was located despite a targeted search. This card's account of PS-G02's content rests on multiple independent, mutually consistent secondary sources (contemporaneous Reuters, Straits Times and Channel News Asia news reporting; Allen & Gledhill, Morgan Lewis and Lexology law-firm client alerts) rather than on independently read primary guideline text. Should be re-fetched and quoted directly once mas.gov.sg is reachable, or via an alternate genuinely primary mirror if one is found.

The Investor Alert List's specific application to unlicensed DPT exchanges -- including the widely-reported 2 September 2021 addition of Binance.com for allegedly providing DPT services without a PS Act licence -- could not be independently confirmed against a primary MAS source this session: mas.gov.sg/investor-alert-list returned the same 'Maintenance' block as every other MAS path tried. This card records the Binance.com episode only as consistently reported by multiple independent secondary media sources (Reuters, The Straits Times, Channel News Asia, The Business Times, Financial Times), not as independently primary-confirmed, consistent with the editorial rule that secondary sources may corroborate but never solely establish a factual claim. Should be re-verified against MAS's own IAL page or a specific enforcement/advisory notice once mas.gov.sg is reachable.

The PS Act's own licence-tier structure for DPT service providers (Standard Payment Institution and Major Payment Institution licence categories, and the volume/threshold criteria distinguishing them, alongside the separate Money-Changing Licence) is stated in this card from consistent secondary/legal-commentary corroboration only. Direct fetch of Singapore Statutes Online's consolidated PS Act text, which would confirm the licence-tier sections (reported as PS Act sections 5-10) verbatim, was not possible this session: sso.agc.gov.sg returned a live-verified HTTP 403 CloudFront block on every path tried, including robots.txt. The Payment Services (Amendment) Bill fetched this session independently confirms 'major payment institution' as a defined term distinct from 'licensee' generally, but does not itself define the licence tiers or their thresholds. Should be re-verified against the consolidated PS Act text directly once sso.agc.gov.sg is reachable.

The Payment Services Act 2019's exact commencement date (28 January 2020) is stated in this card's instruments list as secondary-corroborated background only, consistent with this jurisdiction's other pillar cards, not as independently primary-source-fetched this session.

The original (pre-2021-amendment) text of the PS Act First Schedule's 'digital payment token service' definition (sub-paragraphs (a) and (b)) and of the separately defined 'digital payment token exchange' term was not independently fetched this session: the Payment Services (Amendment) Bill's own clauses confirm these terms' pre-existence and insertion points but, being an amendment, do not reproduce their unchanged text. Should be fetched directly from the consolidated PS Act (sso.agc.gov.sg, currently blocked) or the original Payment Services Bill (2019) if a genuinely fetchable copy can be located.

Whether MAS's 25 September 2025 Guidelines on Standards of Conduct for Digital Advertising Activities (effective 25 March 2026) specifically apply to, supersede, or are intended to operate alongside PS-G02's DPT-specific advertising restrictions was not confirmed this session: the fetched 25 September 2025 media release's own text does not mention digital payment tokens, cryptocurrency, DPT service providers or PS-G02 by name. This card records only that DPT service providers, as MAS-licensed financial institutions, fall within the Guidelines' stated general scope ('all financial institutions and their appointed third parties'), without asserting a specific DPT-targeted effect. Should be independently re-verified once the Guidelines' own text or further MAS guidance addressing this interaction is fetchable.

This pillar covers the DPT-service exchange/trading-platform licensing gate (licence-tier structure, the 2021 amendment's widened 'digital payment token service' definition and 4 April 2024 commencement, business-conduct/consumer-access/listing-policy measures, PS-G02's advertising restrictions, and the Investor Alert List as applied to unlicensed exchanges) only. Custody/safeguarding-of-customer-assets substance and the Financial Services and Markets Act 2022 Part 9 Digital Token Service Provider (DTSP) regime's licensing/scope mechanics are tracked in the Dealing, Custody, Advisory & Management pillar; AML/CFT duties (MAS Notice PSN02, the DTSP regime's AML/CFT rationale and Notice FSM-N27) and enforcement precedent are tracked in the AML/CFT & Enforcement pillar -- not restated here to avoid duplicating citations across pillars.
